topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the abrupt finality of a slammed door. Subject line: Party Cancellation. My thumb hovered over the 'delete' button, a strange, cold knot tightening in my stomach. I reread the message three times, the cheerful invitations and planned decorations mocking the sudden, stark emptiness of the text. I stood by the window, watching the late afternoon sun bleed across the lawn where the streamers were supposed to be. A tremor ran through my hands, making the cheap plastic pen I was holding tremble. Who would cancel something so meticulously planned? A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, a sound that seemed too loud in the quiet apartment. I walked to the kitchen, gripping the edge of the counter until my knuckles were white, needing something tangible to focus on, anything other than the hollow echo of the news.

[story 2]

"No, seriously?" I managed to croak out, the words tasting like dust in my mouth. The phone call had ended moments ago, leaving a ringing silence in its wake that felt deafening. I stared at the screen, the confirmation text glowing accusingly. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I began pacing the length of the living room, my movements jerky, as if my limbs were operating on a different frequency. Each step seemed to land with a dull thud. I stopped abruptly, pressing the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to force the image of the cheerful gathering to rearrange itself into something believable. A tightness constricted my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I needed to check my phone again, to find some misplaced reassurance, but my fingers felt clumsy, unresponsive.

[story 3]

The silence in the room amplified every tiny sound—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—and it all seemed magnified, pressing in on me. I picked up the invitation, the glossy paper feeling flimsy and ridiculous in my grasp. My gaze drifted from the printed script to the space where the RSVP confirmation should have been. A strange, hollow sensation pooled just beneath my ribs. I slowly lowered the paper onto the coffee table, the sound barely audible. I started to rub my arms, a restless, repetitive motion, trying to shake off the peculiar stillness that had taken root. I felt a distinct pressure behind my eyes, a kind of internal static, making the edges of the room seem slightly blurred. I just needed to move, to do anything to disrupt the quiet certainty of the cancellation.
